text: The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneal flap laterally, encountering some resistance at Bagros' space. The flap is extended medially beyond the symphysis pubis, focusing on the medial side due to the direct inguinal hernia.

The surgeon approaches the deep internal ring, revealing the anti-sac of the direct inguinal hernia with visible white lining. Using traction, the surgeon pulls down and away, exposing splayed muscle fibers. Attempts to use scissors are made, but cautery is applied to safely separate the tissue, avoiding the epigastric vessels located up and to the right.

The surgeon dissects inferiorly below Cooper's ligament and the pubic tubercle, extending down to the obturator fossa. The lateral plane is being worked on, with the sac being peeled down off the vas deferens visible to the right of the scissors. The camera is positioned at an oblique angle, revealing the tissue planes that open up with applied traction and counter traction.

The surgeon identifies two bridging veins of the coronamortis near the obturator fossa. The area is inspected for indirect inguinal hernia, while the surgeon pulls up tissue to complete the dissection, searching for a cord lipoma.
The surgeon navigates around the corner, encountering a significant amount of fat on the iliac vessels. The obturator nerve is visible as it is gently pulled out from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon places a Bard 3D Max mesh over the direct hernia defect, ensuring coverage by fixating it in a triangular pattern with sutures at Cooper's ligament, the pubic tubercle, and the high medial portion. The peritoneal flap is then closed.
